segments.ssml: <speak>Quick question. <break time="400ms"/> If I asked you this right now, in English, no subtitles — <emphasis level="moderate">would you freeze</emphasis>, or <emphasis level="strong">would you fire back</emphasis>?</speak>

<speak>Here's the test. I'm going to say three things a native speaker would actually say to you. Don't translate. Just try to answer before I do. Ready? <break time="500ms"/>Number one: <prosody rate="95%">"Sorry, can you say that again?"</prosody> <break time="1200ms"/>Number two: <prosody rate="95%">"What do you think?"</prosody> <break time="1200ms"/>Number three: <prosody rate="95%">"I'll get back to you on that."</prosody></speak>

<speak>I'll tell you when this hit me. <break time="400ms"/>I was in a meeting, my own language, my own field, and someone asked me a question I knew the answer to cold. <break time="400ms"/>I just sat there for two full seconds, the right words stuck somewhere between my brain and my mouth, before I finally got them out. <break time="400ms"/>Everyone noticed the pause. <break time="600ms"/>That's when I learned <emphasis level="moderate">comprehension was never the real problem.</emphasis> The half-second gap between knowing and saying is where conversations actually die, in any language, including your own. <break time="500ms"/>So here's response one, the one that would have saved me that day. When someone repeats something, a native doesn't say <prosody rate="90%">"Repeat, please."</prosody> They say: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="80%">"Sorry, come again?"</prosody></emphasis> <break time="500ms"/>Say that with me right now: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="75%">"Sorry, come again?"</prosody></emphasis></speak>

<speak>Let's build your real kit. Response one, you just got: <emphasis level="moderate">"Sorry, come again?"</emphasis> — for when you didn't catch it. <break time="400ms"/>Response two — someone asks your opinion on the spot: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="85%">"Honestly? I think..."</prosody></emphasis> Just that opener buys you time and sounds completely natural. <break time="400ms"/>Response three — you don't know yet: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="85%">"I'll get back to you on that."</prosody></emphasis> Not <prosody rate="90%">"I don't know."</prosody> That sounds final. This sounds active.</speak>

<speak>Response four — you need to push back, politely: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="85%">"I see your point, but..."</prosody></emphasis> That's not rude. That's how natives disagree without sounding cold. <break time="400ms"/>And response five, the one that saves you in almost any awkward pause: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="85%">"Let me think about that for a second."</prosody></emphasis></speak>

<speak>Two more, because real conversations need more than five moves. <break time="400ms"/>Response six — someone's explaining something fast and you're losing the thread: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="85%">"Wait, can you back up for a second?"</prosody></emphasis> That buys you clarity without sounding lost. <break time="400ms"/>And response seven — you actually agree, but want to add something: <emphasis level="strong"><prosody rate="85%">"Exactly, and on top of that..."</prosody></emphasis> That keeps you in the conversation instead of just nodding.</speak>

<speak>Let's check you fast across all seven. I'll give you the situation, you give me the response — out loud, before I say it. <break time="500ms"/>One: your friend didn't hear what you said. <break time="1000ms"/>Two: your boss asks what you think about the new plan. <break time="1000ms"/>Three: someone asks if you can finish a report by Friday and you're not sure. <break time="1000ms"/>Four: a colleague is talking too fast and you're losing track. <break time="1000ms"/>Five: someone makes a good point and you want to build on it.</speak>

<speak>Now make it yours. Take response two — <emphasis level="moderate">"Honestly? I think..."</emphasis> — and finish it with something true about your own life right now. <emphasis level="moderate">Not my example. Yours.</emphasis> <break time="600ms"/>Say the whole sentence out loud.</speak>

<speak>One more. Take response seven — <emphasis level="moderate">"Exactly, and on top of that..."</emphasis> — and add your own real point to it. <break time="400ms"/>Two personalized sentences, two responses that are actually yours now.</speak>
<speak>If you fired back without translating — <emphasis level="strong">that's activation.</emphasis> That's the whole point.</speak>

<speak>Here's your <emphasis level="moderate">Activation Score.</emphasis> Out of the seven responses, how many did you say out loud, in real time, without freezing? <break time="400ms"/>Give yourself a point for each one. <break time="400ms"/>And here's the real test: which of them will you actually use this week? <break time="500ms"/>Share your Activation Score in the comments and tell me which response you're using today — and stick around, the next drill goes even harder.</speak>
